cases.title: Last Call at Wortfield
cases.scenario:
Wortfield Brewing is a fast-growing craft beer company headquartered in the city of Farlow. Its best-selling product is a limited-edition seasonal Winter IPA sold for 10 weeks each year at **$6.00 per liter**.

Wortfield currently brews the Winter IPA under contract at a large brewery in **Valtona**, far from Farlow — orders must be placed 5 months before the season, and actual output can deviate from the order. Wortfield's own small brewhouse in **Herdal**, next to its home market, brews to order with certainty. Season demand is fixed at 20,000 liters (consumers buy exactly that if available, and no more); the beer is unpasteurized, so unsold liters spoil. Unit economics and Valtona's delivery record are in **Exhibit 1**.

The CEO has asked BCG whether Wortfield should move Winter IPA production from Valtona to its own Herdal brewhouse.

exhibits.title: Winter IPA: unit economics and Valtona delivery record

blocks.name: Valtona actual output, % of order, last 3 seasons
Planning distribution of delivery outcomes (order = 20,000 L)

steps.prompt: Review Exhibit 1. Which of the following analyses would you prioritize in order to understand Wortfield's decision?

options.text: Compare the per-liter production cost in Valtona versus Herdal
Understand how far actual Valtona output can deviate from the ordered volume, and with what probabilities
Quantify the cost of overproduction (spoilage) and underproduction (lost sales) in each scenario
Benchmark Wortfield's marketing spend against other craft brewers
Review the CEO's compensation structure
Estimate how the location choice changes Wortfield's ability to respond to in-season demand

steps.validation: credit if selects all of {1,2,3,6} and none of {4,5}.

steps.prompt: Based on Exhibit 1 and the facts below, which are genuine areas of concern for THIS decision? Select all that apply.

options.text: Chart B shows Valtona output deviating from the order by nearly ±20% in recent seasons (83–118% of order)
Wortfield's head brewer recently won a national award
Unsold Winter IPA spoils and has zero salvage value
Retail price has been stable at $6.00 for three years
Demand forecasters warn that output deviation may widen substantially next season

steps.prompt: Use the planning distribution from Exhibit 1, Chart C (16,000 L — 30% · 20,000 L — 40% · 24,000 L — 30%). Herdal produces exactly 20,000 L. All unit figures — from Table A.

parts.prompt: What is Wortfield's profit on the Winter IPA this season if Valtona produces exactly the correct amount (20,000 liters)? Answer rounded to the nearest whole number, without further explanation or working.
What is the profit if Valtona produces only 16,000 liters (no chance to top up the shortfall)?
What is the profit if Valtona produces 24,000 liters (4,000 more than demand; excess spoils, worthless)?
Given the above, is it more profitable to produce in Valtona or Herdal?

options.text: Valtona
Herdal
It makes no difference
Not enough information to decide

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: Valtona.** Herdal deterministic profit = (6.00−3.25)×20,000 = **$55,000**. Valtona's WORST case ($56,000) already beats Herdal; EV = 0.3×56,000 + 0.4×70,000 + 0.3×60,000 = **$62,800**. Valtona dominates in every scenario.

steps.prompt:
Forecasters now expect much wider deviation next season: **20,000 L (40%), 10,000 L (30%), 30,000 L (30%)** if produced in Valtona. Herdal is unchanged (exactly 20,000 L at $3.25/L).

(Note: expected profit is the probability-weighted average of all possible profit outcomes.)

Based on this, which location has the higher expected profit next season?

options.text: Valtona
Herdal
Both locations have the same expected profit
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: Herdal.**
- Valtona @10,000: 60,000−25,000 = 35,000; @20,000: 70,000; @30,000: 120,000−75,000 = 45,000.
- EV(Valtona) = 0.4×70,000 + 0.3×35,000 + 0.3×45,000 = **$52,000**.
- Herdal = **$55,000** → **flip.** The per-liter margin comparison ($3.50 vs $2.75) points to Valtona in both eras — what flips the answer is the WIDTH of uncertainty interacting with spoilage/lost-sales asymmetry.

steps.prompt: In one line: why did the answer change between Question 3 and Question 4, even though no price or cost changed?
steps.model_answer: Wider output uncertainty raises the expected cost of spoilage and lost sales in Valtona, which outweighs its $0.75/L cost advantage.
steps.validation: credit if mentions (1) uncertainty/deviation got wider AND (2) spoilage/overproduction or lost-sales/shortfall as the profit drain. Both required.

steps.prompt: Besides moving production to Herdal, in what other ways could Wortfield address the issue at hand?

options.text: Negotiate a contract clause that penalizes Valtona for output deviation
Pasteurize or repackage the Winter IPA so unsold volume can be sold after the season
Place a deliberately smaller order in Valtona to reduce spoilage exposure
Increase the marketing budget to grow demand for other product lines
Split production: keep the base volume in Valtona, top up shortfalls locally

steps.prompt:
The BCG partner suggests exactly option 5: keep Valtona production (severe-uncertainty scenario of Question 4), and set up a small "top-up line" at the Herdal brewhouse that brews ONLY when Valtona under-delivers.

- Fixed cost of the top-up line allocated to this season: **$2,500** — incurred no matter what
- Variable cost: **$4.00 per liter** brewed on the top-up line
- If Valtona delivers 10,000 L, the top-up line brews the missing 10,000 L; in other scenarios it brews nothing.

How much is the top-up line expected to increase profit for this SKU this season, taking into account the probabilities in Question 4? Answer rounded to the nearest whole number.
Given the previous questions, would you recommend moving all of Wortfield's Winter IPA production from Valtona to Herdal?

options.text: Yes, Wortfield should move all its production to Herdal
No, Wortfield should not move all its production to Herdal

steps.answer_explain: **Answer: No.** The hybrid (Valtona base + Herdal top-up, EV $55,500) beats both full-Valtona ($52,000) and full-Herdal ($55,000).

steps.prompt: Please explain your answer to Question 8 in two to three lines maximum.
steps.model_answer: Under severe uncertainty full-Herdal beats full-Valtona ($55,000 vs $52,000 expected), but a hybrid — Valtona base plus a Herdal top-up line — captures Valtona's cost advantage while insuring against shortfall, yielding $55,500. So Wortfield should keep Valtona and add the top-up line rather than move everything.
steps.validation: credit if ALL: (1) names the hybrid/top-up as the chosen option, (2) cites at least one of $55,500 / $52,000 / $55,000, (3) gives the logic "cost advantage + insurance against shortfall" in any wording.

steps.prompt: "The CEO of Wortfield joins the call. Please record your final recommendation as you would deliver it — about 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Wortfield should keep Winter IPA production in Valtona and add a small top-up line at the Herdal brewhouse — not move production entirely. Three reasons. First, under next season's demand uncertainty, staying fully in Valtona is worth $52,000 in expected profit, moving fully to Herdal $55,000, but the hybrid is worth $55,500 — the best of the three. Second, the hybrid keeps Valtona's $0.75-per-liter cost advantage while the top-up line insures the roughly one-in-three shortfall scenario. Two risks to manage: internally, running beer production across two sites creates recipe-consistency and quality risk, so we need a common brewing spec and QA protocol before the season; externally, if Valtona's output deviation widens further or its contract terms change, the hybrid math erodes — the top-up line stays attractive only while its fixed cost is under $3,000 a season. Next steps: within 30 days, sign the revised Valtona contract with deviation penalties, and commission the Herdal top-up line by the start of the brewing window.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. An action recommendation appears in the FIRST one–two sentences (keep Valtona + add top-up / hybrid; NOT a full move).
2. At least 2 case numbers are present, **of which at least 1 is an anchor: 55,500 (hybrid EV) OR the 52,000-vs-55,000 comparison** (transcription variants like "fifty five and a half thousand" count).
3. At least 1 internal risk (quality/consistency/two-site operations) AND at least 1 external risk (Valtona deviation/contract, supply, excise) — each tied to the hybrid thesis, not generic.
4. At least 1 time-bound next step.
Fail if: conclusion is built up to at the end instead of stated first, or no anchor number.

cases.title: Watt's the Catch?
cases.scenario:
Ohmhaus is the leading retailer of home EV chargers in the country of Novaria. The city of Arden, its largest market, has a public goal of reaching **10,000 registered electric vehicles by December 31**. Ohmhaus's CMO proposes a Q4 promotion: **if Arden hits the milestone, every charger bought during the quarter is refunded 50% of its price.**

The CMO calls it "an obvious win — 250 extra units at $350 margin, that's $87,500 of extra profit." The CEO has asked BCG to check the math. Key data — in **Exhibit 1**.

exhibits.title: Ohmhaus Q4: baseline and promotion assumptions

blocks.name: Arden EV registrations by quarter (target 10,000 by Dec 31)
blocks.note: illustrative trajectory; use the 30% analyst estimate

steps.prompt:
Select the FEWEST possible items from the list below that are necessary to determine the change in expected profit if Ohmhaus runs the promotion.

(Expected profit is the probability-weighted average of all possible profit outcomes.)

options.text: Variable cost per charger
Probability that Arden reaches the milestone
Quantity demanded under the promotion
Ohmhaus's expected Q4 profit without the promotion, considering all fixed costs
Store rental expense divided by units sold
Any limitation on the number of units eligible for the promotion

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** 1, 2, 3, 6. Fixed costs and rent are unchanged by the promo — irrelevant to the CHANGE in profit. Baseline volume and price are already in Exhibit 1.

steps.prompt: Assume there is no limit on eligible units. What is the CHANGE in expected profit if Ohmhaus runs the promotion (expected profit with promo minus profit without)? Answer rounded to the nearest whole number; a negative answer like -5000 is allowed.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 9500**
- Baseline: 400 × (800−450) = **$140,000**
- Expected revenue per promo unit: 0.7×800 + 0.3×400 = **$680** → expected margin $230
- Promo EV: 650 × 230 = **$149,500** → Δ = **+$9,500**
- The CMO's +$87,500 is **9.2× overstated**: he priced the upside and ignored the expected refund.

steps.prompt: By what percentage would unit sales need to increase (versus 400) so that expected profit is the SAME whether or not the promotion is run? Answer as a percentage rounded to two decimals (e.g. 41.27).
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 52.17**
- Breakeven volume: 140,000 / 230 = 608.6957 → +52.17% vs 400. (First integer volume that beats baseline: 609 units → 609×230 = $140,070.)

steps.prompt: The analysts' 30% is an estimate. At what milestone probability (in %) does the promotion stop adding expected profit, holding demand at 650 units? Answer rounded to two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 33.65**
- Same model as Q2, solved for p: 650 × (350 − 400p) = 140,000 → p* = **33.65%**.
- Current estimate 30% → cushion is only ~3.7 points. **[9b]: порог решён той же EV-моделью, что Q2.**

steps.prompt: Given Questions 2–4, how would you characterize the CMO's "$87,500 obvious win"?

options.text: Correct as stated
Right direction, but the gain is far smaller and fragile to the milestone probability
Wrong direction — the promotion destroys expected profit
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 2. Direction is right (+$9,500 > 0), magnitude is 9.2× overstated, and the gain dies if the probability estimate drifts above 33.65%.

steps.prompt: Beyond the direct profit impact, what other advantages might lead Ohmhaus to run this promotion anyway?

options.text: Ties the brand to Arden's EV milestone and earns earned-media buzz
Builds a customer database of early EV adopters for future services
Reduces Ohmhaus's long-term variable cost per charger
Pulls forward purchases from competitors during a high-visibility quarter

steps.prompt: Before any numbers: the CEO worries about "unlimited refund exposure." In one or two lines, propose ONE mechanism that would limit Ohmhaus's refund exposure while keeping the promotion alive.
steps.model_answer: Cap refund eligibility at a fixed number of units (e.g. the first N sold), or cap the total refund pool at a fixed dollar amount — communicated clearly at point of sale.
steps.validation: credit if proposes a concrete exposure-limiting mechanism (unit cap / dollar-pool cap / per-customer limit / shorter window / partial-refund tier). Fail if answer is "cancel the promotion" or a pure marketing idea with no exposure limit. (Открытый cap-инсайт: кандидат генерит идею сам, ДО готового варианта CFO в Q8.)

steps.prompt:
The CFO lands on exactly that mechanism: cap refund eligibility at the **first 500 units** sold; units beyond 500 are sold at full price. Assume demand stays at 650 units.

What is the change in expected profit versus NOT running the promotion, with this cap? Answer rounded to the nearest whole number.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 27500**
- 500 capped units × $230 expected margin = 115,000; 150 uncapped units × $350 = 52,500 → total $167,500
- Δ vs baseline $140,000 = **+$27,500** (vs +$9,500 uncapped — the cap nearly triples the expected gain).

steps.prompt: Would you recommend running the promotion?

options.text: Yes, uncapped, as the CMO proposed
Yes, but with the 500-unit eligibility cap
No, do not run the promotion
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your final recommendation to Ohmhaus's CEO — about 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Ohmhaus should run the Q4 promotion, but only with the 500-unit eligibility cap — not in the CMO's uncapped form. Three reasons. First, uncapped, the promotion adds only $9,500 of expected profit, not the $87,500 claimed — the CMO ignored the expected refund cost, overstating the gain nine times. Second, the cap limits refund exposure while keeping the demand lift, raising the expected gain to $27,500. Third, the uncapped version is fragile: it stops paying if the milestone probability rises above 33.65%, and analysts already say 30%. Two risks: internally, the cap's fine print can create customer backlash if it isn't communicated clearly at point of sale — we need transparent 'first 500' messaging; externally, consumer-protection rules in Novaria may constrain conditional refunds, so legal must clear the mechanic before launch. Next steps: within two weeks, finalize the capped terms with legal sign-off, and set a tracking dashboard for registrations against the 10,000 milestone.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Action recommendation in the first 1–2 sentences: run WITH cap (not uncapped, not reject).
2. ≥2 case numbers, **of which ≥1 anchor: 27,500 (capped Δ) OR the 9,500-vs-87,500 contrast OR 33.65%**.
3. ≥1 internal risk (cap communication/backlash/execution) AND ≥1 external risk (consumer-protection/regulatory, or probability drift above breakeven) — tied to the thesis.
4. ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if conclusion arrives only at the end, or no anchor number.

cases.title: Cut and Run?
cases.scenario:
Cartford Market is a 60-store grocery chain in the region of Ashvern. Its "Family Dinner Kit" — a ready-to-cook meal kit — is a fixture of the weekly shop; its item economics are in **Exhibit 1**.

The CFO wants to discontinue the kit: "We lose $3 on every kit. Cutting it saves $24,000 a week — the easiest money we'll ever make." The CEO has asked BCG for a view before the next range review.

trigger_phrases: what else do kit buyers buy
exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: Basket analytics: kit-buyer transactions

blocks.name: Average margin per kit-buyer transaction
labels: Other items in the same basket

exhibits.no_spoiler_note: не показывать автоматом — инсайт-1 кандидат должен запросить

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize to evaluate the CFO's proposal?

options.text: Verify the kit's own weekly profit and loss
Measure what ELSE kit buyers purchase in the same basket, and its margin
Estimate how many kit buyers would stop shopping at Cartford if the kit disappeared
Benchmark Cartford's logo and store design against competitors
Review the kit's cost structure for reduction opportunities

steps.prompt: Based on Exhibit 1 and the facts below, which are genuine areas of concern for the discontinuation decision?

options.text: Chart B in Exhibit 1 shows the kit's fully loaded cost rising from $12.20 to $13.00 over two years
The kit's recipe was updated last quarter
Kit sales are evenly spread across the week
Cartford's total store count grew from 58 to 60 this year
The kit ranks #1 in Cartford's customer-satisfaction survey of fresh SKUs

steps.validation: all of {1,5}, none of {2,3,4}. (v3: $9.00 и survey-45% убраны из Q2 — оба спойлерили инсайты; данные приходят через E2-элиситацию и партнёрскую вводную в Q5.)
steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** 1, 5. **Validation:** all of {1,5}, none of {2,3,4}. (v3: $9.00 и survey-45% убраны из Q2 — оба спойлерили инсайты; данные приходят через E2-элиситацию и партнёрскую вводную в Q5.)

parts.prompt: What is the kit's own weekly profit or loss as a standalone item (negative = loss)?
What weekly saving does the CFO's proposal claim, looking at the kit alone?
The CFO's case rests entirely on the kit's own P&L. Before ruling: what ONE thing would you want to know about kit buyers' behavior, in one or two lines?
parts.model_answer: What else kit buyers purchase in the same basket, and the margin on those items.
parts.validation: credit if requests basket contents / other purchases by kit buyers / cross-category margin effect. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → show L1 nudge ("Is the kit's own P&L the whole story of a kit buyer?"), then reveal Exhibit 2 and proceed (линейный флоу сохранён).
trigger_phrases: what else do kit buyers buy
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.prompt: Using Exhibit 2: what is the total weekly margin pool from OTHER items in kit buyers' baskets?

steps.prompt: Based only on Questions 3a–3d, should Cartford discontinue the kit?

options.text: Yes — the kit loses money
No — the attached margin outweighs the loss
It makes no difference
Not enough information to decide

steps.answer_explain: **Answer: Not enough information.** The $72,000 attached pool does NOT all disappear if the kit is cut — only the baskets of customers who actually defect. Without an attribution estimate, neither "yes" nor "no" is provable yet. (Ловушка внутри ловушки: кандидат, поймавший инсайт-1, спешит ответить "No" — и ошибается, потому что ещё не спросил про attribution.)

steps.prompt:
Use the survey: **45% of kit-buying households would move their shop to a competitor** if the kit were discontinued (taking their attached baskets with them); the remaining 55% would keep shopping at Cartford.

What is the NET weekly profit impact of discontinuing the kit? (Negative = Cartford loses money by cutting; e.g. -5000 or 12000.)
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: -8400**
- Save the kit loss: +24,000
- Lose attached margin of defectors: −0.45 × 72,000 = −32,400
- Net = **−$8,400 per week** → cutting the kit makes Cartford POORER. Наивное решение (cut) и correct (keep) расходятся по направлению — flip.

steps.prompt: At what defection rate (in %) would discontinuing the kit be exactly profit-neutral? Answer rounded to two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 33.33**
- Same model as Q5, solved for d: 24,000 = d × 72,000 → d* = **33.33%**. Survey says 45% — comfortably above the line, so KEEP holds. **[9b]: порог из той же модели.**

steps.prompt: The CEO accepts that the kit should stay — but not at a $3-per-kit loss forever. Brainstorm at least SIX distinct ways Cartford could improve the kit's economics without discontinuing it. Structure your ideas.
steps.model_answer:
- Revenue: (1) raise price toward $12 with a value redesign; (2) launch a premium variant at higher margin; (3) bundle the kit with high-margin attach items (wine, dessert) at a combined price.
- Cost: (4) renegotiate ingredient contracts or switch to seasonal inputs; (5) centralize kit assembly in one dark kitchen to cut per-kit labor.
- Basket: (6) loyalty-app targeting so kit promotions go only to high-basket households; (7) use the kit as a paid subscription to lock in weekly trips.
steps.validation: credit if ALL: (1) ≥6 distinct ideas, (2) grouped in ≥2 named categories, (3) ≥1 idea on revenue/price side AND ≥1 on cost side. Fewer than 4 ideas = fail; 4–5 ideas = prompt "push for more" once, then grade.

steps.prompt:
The pricing team models one of these ideas: raise the kit price to **$12.00**, which is expected to cut kit volume by 20% (to 6,400 kits). Attached margin per kit sold stays $9.00, and no households defect (the kit still exists).

What is the change in total weekly kit-ecosystem contribution (kit P&L + attached margin) versus today?
Your final position for the range review:

options.text: Discontinue the kit
Keep the kit as is
Keep the kit and reprice it toward $12
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "The CEO asks for your recommendation before the range review. Record it — about 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Cartford should keep the Family Dinner Kit and reprice it toward $12 — discontinuing it would actively destroy profit. Three reasons. First, cutting the kit doesn't save $24,000 a week; once the 45% of kit households who would defect take their baskets with them, Cartford ends up $8,400 a week worse off. Second, the decision is robust: cutting only breaks even if defection were below 33.3%, and the survey says 45%. Third, repricing to $12 with a 20% volume loss still adds $3,200 a week of ecosystem contribution. Two risks: internally, the survey may overstate true defection — stated intent usually exceeds behavior — so we should A/B a real removal in two test stores before trusting the 45%; externally, a competitor could launch a rival kit and change the defection math entirely. Next steps: run the two-store removal test and the $12 price pilot in parallel over the next eight weeks, then lock the range decision.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Action recommendation in first 1–2 sentences: keep (± reprice), NOT discontinue.
2. ≥2 case numbers, **of which ≥1 anchor: −8,400 (net of cutting) OR 33.33%-vs-45% comparison**.
3. ≥1 internal risk (survey/attribution overstatement, measurement) AND ≥1 external risk (competitor kit, food-cost inflation) — tied to the thesis.
4. ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if conclusion only at the end, or no anchor number.

cases.title: Plane Sight
cases.scenario:
Larkjet is a regional airline in the country of Meridia. On its Astel–Brenmark route it operates **3 daily flights** with 80-seat regional jets. The network team proposes adding a **4th daily frequency**.

Route economics are in **Exhibit 1**. The CFO opposes the idea: "The route already loses money on every departure. A fourth flight digs the hole deeper." The CEO has asked BCG to referee.

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: Cost per flight: $8,400 breakdown
exhibits.no_spoiler_note: строки без классификации — кандидат классифицирует в Q2

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize to evaluate the 4th frequency?

options.text: Separate which costs of a 4th flight are truly incremental versus already-sunk allocations
Forecast demand for the 4th flight, split into new passengers versus passengers shifting from the existing 3 flights
Review the CEO's frequent-flyer status
Check aircraft and crew availability for a 4th rotation
Redesign the airline's livery

parts.prompt: The CFO's "$8,400 loss per departure" is on the table. Before judging the 4th flight: what would you want to know about that $8,400, in one or two lines?
parts.model_answer: Its breakdown — which lines are truly incremental to one extra departure versus fixed allocations of costs we pay anyway.
parts.validation: credit if requests the cost breakdown / decomposition of the $8,400, or asks which costs actually change with an extra flight. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 nudge ("Does the idle jet cost more when it flies?"), then reveal Exhibit 2 and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.prompt: Review Exhibit 2. Which of its cost lines are RELEVANT (i.e., would actually change) for the decision to add the 4th flight? The aircraft that would fly it is already owned and currently idle mid-day.

options.text: Fuel
Crew duty-hour pay
Landing & handling fees
Maintenance reserves
Aircraft ownership (lease)
HQ overhead allocation

parts.answer_explain: **Answer:** 1, 2, 3. Partner confirms after answer: "Correct — the incremental cost of the 4th flight is $1,700 + $900 + $600 = **$3,200**."

steps.prompt: Marginal cost per 4th flight — **$3,200** (confirmed in Q2b from Exhibit 2). New input from the commercial team: the 4th flight would carry **60 passengers**, but **35% of them would simply shift from the existing 3 flights**; 65% are new (incremental) passengers. Average fare $95 (Exhibit 1).

parts.prompt: Using the CFO's fully allocated view: what is the route's profit or loss per existing flight today (negative = loss)?
parts.answer_explain: **Answer: -2700** — 60 × 95 − 8,400 = 5,700 − 8,400 = **−$2,700**. (Это и есть naive-якорь CFO: «каждый вылет убыточен».)

parts.prompt: A junior analyst computes the 4th flight's profit as: all 60 forecast passengers × $95 minus the $3,200 marginal cost. What number does the analyst get?

Now correct the analyst: passengers who merely shift from existing flights bring no NEW revenue to Larkjet (they would have paid anyway), and existing flights lose no cost when they shift. What is the true expected profit impact of the 4th flight, per flight?

steps.prompt: Should Larkjet add the 4th daily frequency?

options.text: No — the route loses $2,700 per flight and a 4th flight makes it worse
Yes — the 4th flight adds about $2,500 per departure
Yes — the 4th flight adds about $505 per departure
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 3. Naive-avg (option 1) отвергает; naive-marginal (option 2) принимает по завышенной величине; correct принимает по honest +505. Знак решения между naive-avg и correct расходится — Trap-action.

steps.prompt: If the 4th flight operates daily all year (365 days), what is its annual profit contribution?
The 35% shift estimate is soft. At what cannibalization rate (in %) does the 4th flight stop adding profit? Answer rounded to two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 43.86**
- Same model as Q3c, solved for c: 60×(1−c)×95 = 3,200 → c* = **43.86%**. Current estimate 35% → the decision survives an ~9-point error. **[9b]: порог из той же модели (marginal, с каннибализацией).**

steps.prompt: Beyond adding the 4th frequency as proposed, in what other ways could Larkjet improve the Astel–Brenmark route's economics?

options.text: Retime the 4th flight to an off-peak slot where cannibalization is lower
Use dynamic pricing to lift average fare on peak departures
Repaint the aircraft
Right-size equipment: deploy a smaller jet on the weakest existing frequency
Sell the marginal seats through a codeshare partner to import incremental demand

steps.prompt: Given all of the above, your recommendation on the 4th frequency:

options.text: Reject — the route is loss-making on a fully allocated basis
Add it — and judge it on incremental economics
Add it only if the route becomes profitable on a fully allocated basis
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Larkjet's CEO — about 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Larkjet should add the fourth daily Astel–Brenmark frequency and judge it on incremental economics — the CFO's fully allocated view is the wrong lens for this decision. Three reasons. First, the $2,700 per-flight 'loss' mixes in aircraft ownership and overhead that don't change whether the idle jet flies or sits; the true marginal cost of the flight is $3,200. Second, after stripping out the 35% of passengers who would merely shift from existing departures, the flight still earns $505 per departure — about $184,000 a year. Third, the call is robust: it only breaks even if cannibalization reached 43.9%, well above the 35% forecast. Two risks: internally, a fourth rotation tightens crew scheduling and maintenance windows, so ops must confirm the duty-roster fits before we commit; externally, a competitor matching the frequency or a fuel-price spike would compress the $505 margin. Next steps: within 30 days, validate the cannibalization estimate from booking-curve data on the existing three flights, and launch the fourth frequency at the start of the next schedule season with a 90-day performance review.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Action recommendation in first 1–2 sentences: ADD the flight on incremental/marginal logic.
2. ≥2 case numbers, **of which ≥1 anchor: 505 (per-flight) OR 184,325 (annual) OR 43.86% breakeven** (transcription variants "five hundred five", "about five hundred" count).
3. ≥1 internal risk (crew/maintenance/ops strain) AND ≥1 external risk (competitor response, fuel, slots) — tied to the thesis.
4. ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if conclusion only at the end, or no anchor number.

cases.title: Lab Partners
cases.scenario:
Serolith Labs runs a network of hospital-affiliated clinical laboratories in the country of Meridia. A national reference laboratory has offered to take over Serolith's entire molecular (PCR) panel volume. Deal economics — in **Exhibit 1**.

The COO is enthusiastic: "We pay $50, they charge $38 — that's $12 a test, $480,000 a year, signed tomorrow." The CEO has asked BCG to pressure-test the deal.

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: The $22 fixed allocation: breakdown per test
exhibits.no_spoiler_note: строки без классификации — кандидат классифицирует в Q2

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize to evaluate the outsourcing offer?

options.text: Split the $22 fixed allocation into costs that disappear if the panel is outsourced versus costs that remain
Assess how the 24h→72h turnaround change affects test volume and referring physicians
Verify the reference lab's quality, accreditation and capacity
Redesign Serolith's patient waiting rooms
Model the annual profit of each option on a consistent basis

parts.prompt: The COO compares $50 fully loaded to the $38 offer. Before judging the deal: what would you want to know about the $22 fixed allocation, in one or two lines?
parts.model_answer: Its breakdown — which components would actually disappear if the panel left, versus costs the network keeps paying regardless.
parts.validation: credit if requests the fixed-cost breakdown / decomposition, or asks which fixed costs actually go away on outsourcing. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 nudge ("Does the building get cheaper when the analyzers leave?"), then reveal Exhibit 2 and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.prompt: Review Exhibit 2. Which of its lines would actually DISAPPEAR if the molecular panel were outsourced?

options.text: Analyzer leases
Molecular team salaries
Building space
Laboratory information system
Accreditation & admin

parts.answer_explain: **Answer:** 1, 2. Avoidable = **$8 per test** (leases $5 + salaries $3); unavoidable = **$14 per test** (building $6 + LIS $4 + accreditation/admin $4) stays either way. Partner confirms the split after answer.

parts.prompt: What annual saving does the COO's "$50 vs $38" logic claim?

Now compare only the costs that actually change. Per test, in-house avoidable cost is $28 variable + $8 avoidable fixed = $36; the outsource price is $38. What is the annual COST impact of outsourcing (negative = costs go UP; e.g. -50000)?
parts.answer_explain: **Answer: -80000** — (36 − 38) × 40,000 = **−$80,000 per year**: outsourcing makes Serolith's cost base $80,000 WORSE, потому что $14/test unavoidable остаётся в здании в любом случае. Наивная экономия $480,000 не просто завышена — знак противоположный.

parts.prompt: The medical team adds a second effect: at a 72-hour turnaround, referring physicians would send an estimated **6% of the panel volume** to a faster competing hospital lab. Under outsourcing, each retained test earns $55 − $38 = $17 of margin. What annual margin does Serolith LOSE from this leaked volume alone? (Positive number.)

steps.prompt:
Putting it together on a consistent annual basis (excluding the unavoidable $14, which is identical in both options):
- KEEP: 40,000 × ($55−$28) − 40,000 × $8 avoidable fixed
- OUTSOURCE: 37,600 retained tests × ($55−$38)

What is the total annual profit impact of outsourcing versus keeping (negative = outsourcing is worse)?
Based on the above, Serolith should:

options.text: Outsource — the reference lab is cheaper per test
Keep the panel in-house
It makes no difference
Not enough information

steps.prompt: Procurement asks for a walk-away number. At what outsource price per test does outsourcing become exactly as profitable as keeping in-house — accounting for both the avoidable-cost structure AND the 6% turnaround leakage? Answer in dollars rounded to two decimals (e.g. 41.27).
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 34.79**
- Full-model breakeven: 37,600 × (55 − p) = 760,000 → p* = **$34.79**
- Sanity: at $34 outsourcing wins ($789,600 vs $760,000); at $35 it loses ($752,000) — the threshold sits between them.
- **[9b]:** порог решён ТОЙ ЖЕ полной моделью (avoidable + leakage). Cost-only порог был бы $36, наивный — $50; оба неверны. (v2: формулировка two-decimals — спор $34/$35 для грейдера снят.)

steps.prompt: Name ONE advantage of keeping the panel in-house that is NOT captured in the cost model above, in one line only.
steps.model_answer: Control of quality, turnaround and clinical relationships keeps Serolith strategically sticky with referring physicians (or: retains molecular capability for future test-menu expansion).
steps.validation: credit if names a non-modeled qualitative advantage (quality control / physician relationships / capability retention / data ownership / surge flexibility). Fail if repeats a modeled number (cost, leakage).

steps.prompt: If Serolith nonetheless pursued outsourcing at some future price, what would be the key risks to consider?

options.text: Turnaround and quality slippage beyond the assumed 6% leakage
Dependence on a single reference lab's pricing power at renewal
Loss of in-house molecular capability that is costly to rebuild
The lab building's owned space becoming stranded cost
Patients preferring the reference lab's brand

steps.prompt: Your recommendation to the CEO:

options.text: Sign the $38 deal as proposed
Decline at $38; revisit only below the walk-away price and with a turnaround SLA
Outsource half the volume as a compromise
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your final recommendation to Serolith's CEO — about 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Serolith should decline the outsourcing offer at $38 and keep the molecular panel in-house — the deal would make the company worse off, not save money. Three reasons. First, the claimed $480,000 saving compares the $38 price to a $50 fully loaded cost, but $14 of that is building, LIS and accreditation cost that stays either way; on the costs that actually change, outsourcing RAISES the cost base by $80,000 a year. Second, the slower 72-hour turnaround would leak about 6% of volume to a faster competitor, costing another $40,800 — in total, outsourcing destroys $120,800 of annual profit. Third, the walk-away math: outsourcing only becomes attractive below roughly $34.79 a test, so at $38 there is nothing to negotiate at the margin. Two risks to manage: internally, keeping the panel means we must hold onto scarce molecular staff — a retention plan for that team is needed now; externally, the reference lab may return with a price under $35, and payer reimbursement for PCR panels could fall, which would reopen the case. Next steps: within two weeks, respond to the reference lab with the sub-$35 threshold and a 24-hour SLA condition, and start the molecular-team retention review in parallel.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Action recommendation in first 1–2 sentences: decline at $38 / keep in-house.
2. ≥2 case numbers, **of which ≥1 anchor: −120,800 (total impact) OR the $34.79 (~$34–35) walk-away threshold**.
3. ≥1 internal risk (staff retention / capability) AND ≥1 external risk (reference-lab repricing, payer reimbursement) — tied to the thesis.
4. ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if conclusion only at the end, or no anchor number.

cases.title: Suite Spot
cases.scenario: Veldmere Hotels operates a 200-room business hotel in the city of Corvan, open 365 nights a year. The commercial team proposes cutting the public room rate from **$140 to $120**, forecasting occupancy to rise from **60% to 74%**. Economics — **Exhibit 1**. The CEO: "Cheaper rooms, fuller hotel — but are we just buying occupancy with margin? Check it properly."

exhibits.title: Veldmere Corvan: current economics and proposal

blocks.name: Room-nights per night, current
rows: Corporate block (separate master account, NOT in Table A figures)
blocks.note: Table A figures cover the public room business only

blocks.name: Occupancy by month, last 12 months
blocks.note: illustrative within 54-66% band; average 60%; no month above 70%

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: Corporate master agreement: key terms

blocks.name: Terms
rows: resets to public+$15 ONLY if public < $110

steps.prompt: Review Exhibit 1. Which analyses would you prioritize?

options.text: Compare the proposal on CONTRIBUTION per available room-night, not just revenue
Check whether the public rate cut affects the corporate negotiated segment
Verify capacity: can the hotel physically absorb 74% occupancy
Redesign the hotel lobby
Test how sensitive the result is to the occupancy forecast

steps.prompt: Based on Exhibit 1, which observations are relevant to evaluating the proposal?

options.text: Chart C shows occupancy never exceeded 70% in 12 months — the 74% forecast is above any observed month
The hotel has 200 rooms
A separate corporate block of 36 room-nights exists outside the Table A figures — a public-rate cut COULD interact with it through its contract terms
The hotel is open 365 nights

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: 70.30** — 0.74 × (120 − 25). Both views point the same way — so far.

steps.prompt: What is the annual change in contribution from the proposal? Nearest whole number.
steps.answer_explain: **Answer: 94900** — 200 × 365 × (70.30 − 69.00) = **+$94,900/yr**. (Revenue view: +$350,400 — same direction, smaller true magnitude.)

parts.prompt: The numbers look positive. Before you sign off: what could still reverse this, and what would you ask to see?
parts.model_answer: Whether the public cut leaks into the corporate negotiated rate (contract terms / parity clauses), and how robust the result is to the occupancy forecast.
parts.validation: credit if names corporate-rate interaction / contract terms OR forecast sensitivity as a check to run. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("Does anyone in this hotel pay a rate that isn't the public rate?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.prompt: Which checks will you actually run before confirming the recommendation?

options.text: Whether $120 triggers the parity clause
Breakeven occupancy at the $120 rate
Whether the CEO prefers the old rate
Whether forecast 74% exceeds any physical/operational capacity limit

parts.answer_explain: **Answer:** 1, 2, 4. Capacity check: 74% × 200 public + 36 corporate = 184 ≤ 200 rooms ✓. **Validation:** all of {1,2,4}, none of {3}.

steps.prompt: Per Exhibit 2: below what public rate would the corporate segment reprice? Whole dollars.
steps.answer_explain: **Answer: 110** — at the proposed $120 the clause is NOT triggered; corporate revenue is unchanged. The scent does not turn into a trap — and you can prove it.

steps.prompt: At the $120 rate, what occupancy makes the proposal exactly contribution-neutral versus today? Percent, two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 72.63**
- Same model as Q4: occ* = 69.00 / (120 − 25) = **72.63%**. Forecast 74% clears it by only 1.37pp — the recommendation is right, AND fragile to the forecast. **[9b]: порог той же contribution-моделью.**

steps.prompt: Your conclusion on the rate cut:

options.text: No — hidden corporate cannibalization reverses the gain
No — the RevPAR gain is a revenue illusion; contribution falls
Yes — the numbers hold on both revenue and contribution views; parity is untriggered at $120; monitor the thin occupancy cushion
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 3. Оба соблазнительных «No» — ложные развороты; кандидат обязан их отклонить С ЦИФРАМИ.

steps.prompt: In one line: why can you be confident this recommendation won't reverse under scrutiny?
steps.model_answer: The naive and full calculations agree in direction (+$350,400 revenue, +$94,900 contribution), and both candidate reversal mechanisms — parity ($110 trigger vs $120) and breakeven occupancy (72.63% vs 74%) — fail to fire when checked.
steps.validation: credit if states (1) both views agree AND (2) names at least one checked-and-cleared reversal mechanism.

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Veldmere's CEO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Veldmere should implement the rate cut to $120 — and I can confirm this is genuinely positive, not an illusion. I checked it three ways. First, it holds on the economics that matter: contribution, not just revenue — the hotel gains $94,900 a year in contribution, on top of the $350,400 revenue-view gain, so both paths agree. Second, I checked the most dangerous reversal: the corporate master agreement reprices only if the public rate falls below $110, so at $120 the 36 corporate room-nights are untouched. Third, the breakeven occupancy at the new rate is 72.63% against a 74% forecast — the recommendation is right but the cushion is only 1.4 points, and Chart C shows no month above 70% yet. So: implement, with a monthly occupancy trigger — if we run below 73% for two consecutive months, revisit the rate. Risks: internally, the forecast itself is the weak joint — revenue management should re-underwrite the 74% with booking-pace data within 30 days; externally, a competitor matching the cut would compress the occupancy gain. Next step: launch at the start of next quarter with the monitoring trigger in place.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first 1–2 sentences: implement / yes.
2. **Clean-якорь: подтверждение ОБОИХ путей** — упомянуты и revenue/RevPAR-gain, и contribution-gain (94,900 или 350,400-vs-94,900), словами «both agree / сходятся» в любом виде.
3. ≥1 проверенный-и-отклонённый механизм разворота с цифрой: parity $110-trigger ИЛИ breakeven 72.63%-vs-74%.
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск, привязанные к тезису; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if: рекомендация только в конце, или кандидат «нашёл» разворот, которого нет, или ни одного проверенного механизма.

cases.title: Churn After Reading
cases.scenario: Ozmira is a video-streaming service in the country of Meridia with **1,000,000 subscribers** at **$10/month**. The CFO proposes raising the price 20% to **$12**: "One million subs, two dollars each — that's two million a month, effective immediately." Base mix and behavioral assumptions — **Exhibits 1–2**.

blocks.name: Base
rows: 400,000 (locked until renewal; all face renewal by month 12)

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize to pressure-test the CFO's "$2M immediately"?

options.text: Split the base by plan type: who is actually repriced on day one
Model the churn response of the monthly segment over 12 months
Model the renewal decision of the annual segment at the new price
Compare Ozmira's logo to competitors'
Define the comparison metric: month-12 run-rate versus today's, both scenarios

steps.prompt: Per Exhibit 1: how much of the CFO's "$2M from day one" arrives in month 1?

options.text: All of it — the price applies to 1,000,000 subs
At most $1.2M — only the 600,000 monthly subs are repriced immediately; the annual base is locked until renewal
None of it
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 2. Инсайт-1: annual-mix маскирует и немедленный эффект, и будущий renewal-шок.

steps.prompt: WITH the hike: total month-12 run-rate (monthly segment from Q3d + annual segment at the new price with 20% non-renewal)?
Comparing month-12 run-rates: what did the hike actually do?

options.text: Added about $2M/month, as claimed
Added revenue, but less than claimed
REDUCED the month-12 run-rate by about $169K/month
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 3. Δ = 8,435,731.2 − 8,604,600 = **−$168,869**. Naive +$2M → correct −$169K: направление перевёрнуто.

steps.prompt: The 20% annual non-renewal is an estimate. At what annual non-renewal rate (in %) does the hike become month-12-neutral, holding the monthly-segment response fixed? Two decimals.
Besides the flat +20% as proposed, how else could Ozmira capture more revenue?

options.text: Grandfather existing subs; apply $12 to new subscribers only
Raise the annual plan first (locked base, lower immediate churn exposure)
Introduce an ad-supported cheaper tier to catch price-driven churners
Increase the logo size in the app
Stage the hike ($1 now, $1 in 12 months) to soften the churn shock

steps.prompt: Your recommendation on the CFO's proposal:

options.text: Implement the +20% as proposed
Reject any price increase
Do not implement as proposed — restructure the increase (staged / grandfathered / tiered) to manage the churn and renewal response
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Ozmira's CFO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Ozmira should not implement the flat 20% increase as proposed — by month twelve it would leave monthly revenue about $169,000 LOWER, not $2 million higher. Two things break the CFO's math. First, the mix: only the 600,000 monthly subscribers are repriced on day one — the 400,000 annual subs are locked until renewal, and at renewal a fifth of them walk at the new price. Second, the churn compounding: with elevated churn at 3% a month, the monthly cohort's 12-month survival drops from 0.83 to 0.69, so the segment shrinks faster than the price rises. The decision is also fragile the other way: the hike only breaks even if annual non-renewal stays under 16.5%, against a 20% research estimate. Instead, restructure: grandfather existing subscribers or stage the increase, and lead with the annual plan where the churn exposure is lowest. Risks: internally, our churn-response estimates come from one research study — validate with a 5% pricing A/B before any rollout; externally, a competitor could hold price and turn our hike into their acquisition campaign. Next step: run the A/B test this quarter and bring back a staged pricing plan.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first 1–2 sentences: do NOT implement as proposed (restructure ≠ reject entirely).
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: −168,869 (≈−169K) ИЛИ контраст +2M-vs-минус, ИЛИ 16.48%**.
3. Оба механизма названы: annual-lock/mix И churn/компаундинг (любыми словами).
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск к тезису; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail if вывод только в конце или нет якоря.

cases.title: Return to Sender
cases.scenario: Parcelvane runs last-mile parcel delivery across three zones in the region of Ostwick: **Urban, Suburban, Rural**. Flat customer price **$6.00 per parcel**. The CFO wants to exit Rural: "It costs us $7 a parcel against $6 of revenue — closing it saves $10,000 a week." Zone economics — **Exhibit 1**.

exhibits.title: Cost per parcel by zone vs price

blocks.name: Cost per parcel (variable + allocated fixed) vs $6.00 price

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: The $180,000/week network fixed cost

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize?

options.text: Determine how much of the $180,000 fixed cost actually disappears if Rural closes
Compute Rural's direct contribution (price minus variable cost)
Understand how Rural's closure affects customers who use Parcelvane across zones
Repaint the delivery vans
Recompute the remaining zones' allocated cost after a closure

steps.prompt: From Exhibit 1, which statements are correct?

options.text: Rural is the only zone whose FULL cost exceeds the $6.00 price
Rural's VARIABLE cost ($5.20) is still below the $6.00 price — it covers its own delivery cost
Urban's variable cost is the highest
The $1.80 allocation is a cash cost caused by each zone

parts.prompt: The $10,000 "saving" rests on the $1.80 allocation. What would you want to know about the $180,000 before deciding?
parts.model_answer: Its breakdown — which components actually stop being paid if Rural closes, versus trunk/depot costs that remain and simply re-spread over fewer parcels.
parts.validation: credit if requests the fixed-cost breakdown / asks what is avoidable on closure. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("Does the trunk linehaul get cheaper when Rural's parcels leave it?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: 1000** — avoidable 9,000 − lost contribution 8,000 = **+$1,000/week**. Не $10,000 — но всё ещё плюс: направление наива пока держится.

steps.prompt: Based on Questions 1–4 alone, should Parcelvane close Rural?

options.text: Yes — it saves $10,000 a week
Yes — it saves about $1,000 a week
No — closing loses money
Not enough information — the effect of closure on the REST of the network is unexamined

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 4. Ловушка-в-ловушке: «Yes, $1,000» — честный ответ по cost-side, но commercial-сторона не проверена. Кандидат обязан спросить, что закрытие делает с остальной сетью.

steps.prompt: Commercial input: **three anchor e-commerce clients buy Parcelvane as a full-coverage network**. If Rural closes, they estimate moving **5% of Urban+Suburban volume** to a full-coverage competitor. Using each zone's contribution per parcel, what weekly contribution is at risk? Nearest whole number.
Total weekly effect of closing Rural, all-in (negative = closing loses money)?
At what share of core (Urban+Suburban) volume at risk does closing become exactly neutral? Percent, two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 0.38**
- Same full model: s* = 1,000 / (90,000 × 2.9333) = **0.38%**. Клиенты оценивают 5% — решение «закрыть» выживает только при риске в 13 раз меньше минимальной правдоподобной оценки. Гиперчувствительность = сам инсайт. **[9b].**

steps.prompt: Your recommendation:

options.text: Close Rural — the zone is loss-making
Keep Rural — and treat it as the price of the network contract with anchor clients; fix its variable cost separately
Close Rural but keep the micro-depot
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Parcelvane's CEO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Parcelvane should keep the Rural zone — closing it would cost about $12,200 a week, not save $10,000. The CFO's number dies twice. First, on costs: of the $1.80 allocation, only the rural spoke and micro-depot — $9,000 a week — actually stop being paid; against Rural's $8,000 of direct contribution, closing nets just $1,000, not $10,000. Second, and decisively, on the network: our three anchor e-commerce clients buy full coverage, and even their conservative 5% shift of core volume costs $13,200 a week of contribution — thirteen times more than the $1,000 cost-side gain. The closure only makes sense if less than 0.38% of core volume were at risk, which no one believes. So: keep Rural, and attack its real problem — the $5.20 variable cost — through route densification or a local delivery partner. Risks: internally, keeping a high-cost zone without a cost program cements the loss, so ops must deliver a variable-cost plan; externally, if an anchor client renegotiates rates, the network-value math changes and we should re-run it. Next steps: 90-day rural cost-reduction sprint, and lock the three anchor contracts to multi-year terms this quarter.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: keep Rural.
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: −12,200 ИЛИ 0.38%-vs-5% ИЛИ контраст 10,000→1,000→−12,200**.
3. Оба слоя названы: avoidable-vs-allocated И network/anchor-client риск.
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail если вывод в конце или нет якоря.

cases.title: No Pain, No Gain?
cases.scenario:
Repstead Fitness runs 20 gyms in the region of Meridale with **50,000 members at $40/month** — exactly at its policy capacity of 2,500 members per gym (insurance and service-quality cap). There is a waitlist of prospective **premium members at $55/month** (expected 8 visits/month). The regulator has also been pressing gym chains on auto-renewal fairness for inactive members.

The CFO proposes: terminate the auto-renewal of the **15,000 dormant members** and onboard **15,000 premium members** from the waitlist: "Fifteen dollars more per member, cleaner billing, and the regulator off our backs." Member economics — **Exhibit 1**.

blocks.name: Average visits per member per month

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: Premium proposal & capacity policy

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize?

options.text: Compute margin per member BY SEGMENT: price minus visits × usage cost
Compare dormant-member margin to premium-member margin — the actual swap
Assess how a forced purge affects the RETAINED members' behavior
Choose new dumbbell colors
Verify the regulatory exposure the CFO cites

steps.prompt: From Exhibit 1, which observations matter for the swap decision?

options.text: Dormant members average 0.5 visits — they consume almost no usage cost
Active members average 12 visits — at $2.50 a visit they carry $30/month of usage cost
The chain has 20 gyms
The base is exactly at the 50,000 policy cap — any premium onboarding requires freeing slots

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: 35.00** — 55 − 8 × 2.50. Dormant-член на **$3.75/mo маржинальнее** premium-члена, хотя платит на $15 меньше.

parts.prompt: Before pricing the swap: what would you want to see about the premium proposal and the capacity rule?
parts.model_answer: The premium members' expected usage economics and whether the capacity cap truly forces a one-for-one swap (or can be expanded).
parts.validation: credit if asks about premium usage/economics OR the capacity/cap mechanics. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("What does a premium member cost the gym floor?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: -56250** — 15,000 × (35.00 − 38.75) = **−$56,250/month**. Инсайт-1: своп меняет самый маржинальный сегмент на менее маржинальный.

steps.prompt: Member research adds: a forced purge of dormants raises churn among CASUAL members by **2 percentage points** for the following period (brand backlash: "they kick you out when you don't come"). At Casual margin per member, what monthly margin is at risk?
Total monthly effect of the swap, all-in (negative = the swap destroys margin)?
steps.answer_explain: **Answer: -68250** — −56,250 − 12,000 = **−$68,250/month**. Наив обещал +225,000 выручки; по марже — минус. Флип.

steps.prompt: At what premium PRICE does the swap become exactly margin-neutral, holding premium usage at 8 visits and including the adverse-retention effect? Dollars, two decimals.

The CEO accepts the swap is value-destroying — but the capacity pressure, the premium waitlist and the regulator are all real. Brainstorm at least SIX distinct ways to address them WITHOUT purging dormant members. Structure your ideas.
steps.model_answer:
- Capacity: (1) off-peak premium tier — sell premium access restricted to non-congested hours (dormants don't congest anyway); (2) expand capacity via 1–2 new sites in waitlist-dense areas; (3) dynamic class scheduling to lift effective peak throughput.
- Revenue: (4) price premium at $60+ (above the $59.55 breakeven) and onboard against natural attrition instead of purges; (5) convert dormants to a cheaper "digital + 2 visits" tier — keeps margin, frees insurance-counted slots voluntarily.
- Regulatory: (6) proactive fair-billing program — annual usage statements + one-click pause for dormants (kills the fine exposure without killing the members); (7) opt-in re-confirmation of auto-renew (compliance by consent, retention preserved).
steps.validation: credit if ALL: ≥6 distinct ideas; ≥2 named categories; ≥1 идея на capacity И ≥1 на regulatory. <4 идей = fail; 4–5 = один push, затем грейд.

steps.prompt: If Repstead nonetheless piloted a limited swap, key risks to consider?

options.text: Adverse-retention spreading beyond Casual into Active members
Premium members' ACTUAL usage exceeding 8 visits, compressing the $35 margin further
Regulator viewing mass termination itself as unfair practice
Dumbbells rusting faster

steps.prompt: Your recommendation:

options.text: Execute the swap — revenue per member rises
Reject the swap; solve capacity and compliance without purging (premium at ≥$59.55 against natural attrition, fair-billing program for dormants)
Purge only half the dormants
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Repstead's CEO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Repstead should reject the dormant-for-premium swap — it would destroy about $68,250 of margin every month, not add value. The CFO's fifteen-dollar revenue gain hides two losses. First, margin: a dormant member at half a visit a month is our most profitable segment at $38.75, while a premium member at eight visits nets only $35 — so each swap loses $3.75, or $56,250 a month across fifteen thousand members. Second, behavior: a forced purge lifts casual-member churn by two points, costing another $12,000 a month. The swap only breaks even if premium were priced at $59.55, and the waitlist is at $55. Instead: solve capacity and compliance directly — price a premium off-peak tier above $60 and onboard against natural attrition, and replace the purge with a fair-billing program — usage statements and one-click pause — which removes the $150,000 regulatory exposure without killing the members who fund the gym. Risks: internally, premium members' real usage may exceed eight visits and compress margin further, so pilot with usage tracking; externally, the regulator may still mandate changes to auto-renewal, so legal should pre-clear the fair-billing design. Next step: launch the fair-billing program within 60 days and a priced-up premium pilot in the three most waitlisted gyms.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: reject the swap.
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: −68,250 ИЛИ 38.75-vs-35.00 ИЛИ $59.55-vs-$55**.
3. Оба механизма: usage-cost margin И adverse-retention.
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail если вывод в конце или нет якоря.

cases.title: Seat of the Pants
cases.scenario: Taskspan sells B2B workflow software to **200 clients** at **$30 per seat per month**. The board pushes a move to usage-based pricing at **$0.03 per operation**: "Usage is the future — price follows value, and our heaviest users finally pay for what they consume. Migrate everyone." Client base — **Exhibit 1**.

blocks.name: Clients by monthly operations volume
blocks.note: Segment A has seat-compliance friction (login sharing, audits)

exhibits.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed
exhibits.title: Per-client monthly bill: seat vs usage @ $0.03/op

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize?

options.text: Compute current seat revenue and proposed usage revenue BY SEGMENT, not just in total
Identify which segments pay more and which pay less under the new model
Assess each segment's likely reaction (churn/acceptance) to its bill change
Rebrand the product
Determine the usage rate at which a full migration would be revenue-neutral

steps.prompt: From Exhibit 1, which observations are decision-relevant?

options.text: Usage intensity varies 10× across segments (60,000 vs 6,000 ops) — a single pricing model will move money between them
Segment A has seat-compliance friction — they may ACCEPT a higher usage bill that removes audits
Taskspan has 200 clients
Segment C is the largest by client count

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: -66600** — 176,400 − 243,000 = **−$66,600/month**. Наивное действие хуже, чем ничего не делать.

parts.prompt: Before killing or blessing the migration: what comparison would you want to see?
parts.model_answer: Each segment's current seat bill versus its usage bill — who wins and who loses per client.
parts.validation: credit if requests per-segment / per-client bill comparison under both models. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("Does every client's bill move the same direction?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

parts.prompt: Per Exhibit 2, which statements are true?

options.text: Only Segment A pays MORE under usage pricing
Segments B and C would receive a large price CUT for no strategic reason
Migrating B and C is where the $66,600 loss lives
Usage pricing raises every client's bill

steps.prompt: Alternative action: migrate ONLY Segment A (they accept — usage pricing ends their seat audits) and keep B and C on seats. Monthly revenue change versus today?
steps.answer_explain: **Answer: 18000** — A: 108,000 − 90,000 = **+$18,000/month**. Selective threshold-migration бьёт и наив (−66,600), и статус-кво.

steps.prompt: At what usage rate (cents per operation, two decimals) would a FULL migration be exactly revenue-neutral?
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 4.13**
- Same model: total ops = 60×60,000 + 90×22,000 + 50×6,000 = 5,880,000 → r* = 243,000 / 5,880,000 = $0.0413/op = **4.13 cents**. Предложенные 3¢ ниже порога → полный переход теряет деньги при любой сегментации спроса. **[9b].**

steps.prompt: Key risks of the SELECTIVE (A-only) migration?

options.text: Perceived unfairness: two pricing models across the client base
Segment-A usage volatility: their bill now swings with volume
B/C clients demanding the (cheaper-for-them) usage model
The office plants wilting

steps.prompt: Your recommendation:

options.text: Migrate everyone, as the board proposed
Stay fully on seat pricing
Migrate only above-threshold usage clients (Segment A); keep B and C on seats; revisit the rate before any wider rollout
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Taskspan's board — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Taskspan should migrate only its heavy-usage segment to usage-based pricing and keep the rest on seats — the board's 'migrate everyone' would cut revenue by $66,600 a month, worse than doing nothing. The average hides the story: at three cents an operation, only Segment A's bill rises — plus $300 a client, which they'll accept because it ends their seat audits — while Segments B and C would get five-hundred- and seven-hundred-dollar price cuts for no strategic reason; that's where the loss lives. Selective migration of A alone adds $18,000 a month. And the rate itself is miscalibrated: full migration only breaks even at 4.13 cents per operation, well above the proposed three. Risks: internally, running two pricing models invites fairness pushback, so we need clear packaging — usage as a premium 'no-audit' plan, not a discount; externally, B and C may demand the cheaper usage model, so the public usage rate must be set at or above the 4.13-cent neutral point before anyone else migrates. Next step: pilot the A-segment migration next quarter with usage-volatility caps, and re-price the usage rate before any wider rollout.

Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: selective / A-only migration (не «всех», не «никого»).
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: −66,600-vs-+18,000 контраст ИЛИ 4.13¢**.

Fail если вывод в конце или нет якоря.

cases.title: Ticket to Rise
cases.scenario: Marquestone Cinemas operates 12 theatres in the region of Velbray. The pricing team proposes raising the standard ticket from **$10 to $12**. Demand research predicts attendance would fall **15%**. The CFO has run the numbers himself: "Even at minus fifteen percent, ticket profit still goes UP — I checked the elasticity. Approve it." steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize to check the CFO's approval?

Determine what a LOST visitor takes with them beyond the ticket
Compute the full-model breakeven attendance drop
Redesign the cinema logo
Check how concession purchasing relates to attendance

steps.prompt: From Exhibit 1, which observations matter?

options.text: Concessions are over a third of total revenue — and they walk in through the same door as tickets
Ticket margin is 50% — half of every ticket dollar goes to studios
Marquestone has 12 theatres
The proposed price is $12

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: 16.67** — 1 − 200,000/240,000. Наивный вывод: «ожидаем 15%, запас до 16.67% — поднимаем». Ловушка выглядит закрытой.

parts.prompt: The ticket math survives its own stress test. Before approving: what does Marquestone lose WITH each lost visitor, beyond the ticket?
parts.model_answer: Their concession spend — attach rate, average spend and margin per visitor.
parts.validation: credit if names concession/food&beverage economics per visitor. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("What else is in a moviegoer's hands besides the ticket?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

steps.prompt: Weekly concession margin LOST with the predicted 15% attendance drop? Nearest whole number.
Total weekly profit impact of the price rise, all-in (negative = worse)?
At what attendance drop (%) does the price rise break even on the FULL model (tickets + concessions)? Two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 9.84**
- Same full model, solved for d: (240,000(1−d) − 200,000) − 40,000d×4.16 = 0 → d* = 40,000/406,400 = **9.84%**. Три числа: истинный порог 9.84% < ожидание 15% < наивный порог 16.67% — решение живёт между порогами двух моделей. **[9b].**

steps.prompt: Alternative moves to capture revenue without the full-price hit?

options.text: Raise price only on peak shows (Fri–Sat evening), keep off-peak at $10
Bundle ticket + concession combo at a premium price to protect attach
Introduce a subscription pass to lock frequency of high-attach visitors
Print larger tickets

steps.prompt: Your recommendation:

options.text: Approve the $12 across the board — ticket elasticity supports it
Reject any price change
Do not approve as proposed — the concession leak makes it value-destroying; use targeted/peak pricing and bundles instead
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Marquestone's CFO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Marquestone should not approve the across-the-board rise to $12 — it would cost about $20,960 a week, even though the CFO's ticket math is completely correct. That's the point: his elasticity check is right inside its own perimeter — ticket margin does rise $4,000 a week and survives up to a 16.7% attendance drop. But every lost visitor also takes $4.16 of concession margin out the door — 65% attach at $8 and 80% margin — and at the predicted 15% drop that's $24,960 of high-margin food and beverage gone. On the full model the price rise only works if attendance falls less than 9.84%, and the research says 15%. Instead, capture the pricing power where it doesn't bleed: peak-show pricing, ticket-plus-combo bundles that protect attach, and a subscription pass for high-frequency visitors. Risks: internally, the 15% elasticity estimate itself should be validated with a two-theatre price pilot before any rollout; externally, a streaming release-window shift could move attendance independently of our pricing and muddy the read. Next step: launch the pilot in two theatres next month with concession tracking built into the readout.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: do NOT approve as proposed (targeted pricing ≠ reject all pricing).
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: −20,960 ИЛИ тройка порогов 9.84/15/16.67 ИЛИ 4.16**.
3. Названо, что наивная математика ВЕРНА в своём периметре, но неполна (concession leak).
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail если вывод в конце или нет якоря.

Chalk shortages

cases.title: The Fast and the Spurious
cases.scenario: Fleetrow rents cars across the region of Ostane with a fleet of **1,800 vehicles**, replaced every **36 months**. The commercial director proposes moving to a **24-month cycle**: "A two-year-old car resells at $21,000 against $15,600 for a three-year-old — that's $5,400 more on every car we sell. At nine hundred disposals a year, we're leaving almost five million on the table." Fleet economics — **Exhibit 1**.

steps.prompt: Which analyses would you prioritize?

Repaint the rental offices

steps.prompt: What is wrong with the director's "$5,400 × 900 disposals" frame?

options.text: Nothing — it correctly values the proposal
It counts only the selling side of the cycle: faster cycles also mean buying new cars sooner and swapping more often
The resale figures are wrong
Not enough information

parts.answer_explain: **Answer: 375.00** — (30,000 − 21,000) / 24. Быстрый цикл реально дешевле — но на $25/машину/мес, не на $5,400/продажу.

steps.prompt: Each swap takes the car out of service for **6 days** (daily contribution **$45**) and costs **$250** in refurbishment/logistics. The faster cycle adds **300 swaps a year** (900 − 600). What is the total annual cost of the extra swaps?
Net annual benefit of the 24-month cycle, all-in?
steps.answer_explain: **Answer: 384000** — 540,000 − 156,000. Направление директора верное; величина — **×12.66 меньше** заявленных 4.86M.

steps.prompt: Used-car prices move. At what 24-month resale value does the faster cycle become exactly neutral? Dollars, two decimals.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 20573.33**
- Same full model, solved for v: 21,600 × (400 − (30,000−v)/24) = 156,000 → v* = **$20,573.33**. Подушка всего $426.67/машина — решение верное И хрупкое к рынку подержанных. **[9b].**

steps.prompt: Key risks to the 24-month program?

options.text: A used-car price correction below ~$20,573 flips the economics
Swap-driven downtime clustering in peak season
New-vehicle supply/lead-time constraints at doubled purchasing volume
Customers disliking newer cars

steps.prompt: Your recommendation:

options.text: Approve, and book ~$4.9M of annual benefit
Approve — but plan for ~$384K of annual benefit, with a resale-price trigger at $20,573
Reject — the faster cycle loses money
Not enough information

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Fleetrow's board — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Fleetrow should adopt the 24-month cycle — but plan for about $384,000 a year of benefit, not the five million in the proposal. The direction is right and the size is wrong by a factor of twelve. The $5,400-per-car frame counts only the selling side; on a full cycle view, faster replacement saves $25 per car per month of depreciation — $540,000 a year across the fleet — and then gives back $156,000 in the three hundred extra swaps: six days of downtime and $250 of refurbishment each. And the remaining benefit is fragile: it breaks even if two-year resale values fall below $20,573 — just $427 under today's $21,000. So: approve, size it honestly, and wire in a resale-price trigger that pauses accelerated disposals if auction values slip under the threshold. Risks: internally, doubled purchasing volume strains vehicle supply and swap logistics, so procurement must confirm lead times before the ramp; externally, the used-car market itself is the single point of failure — hedge by staggering disposals across quarters. Next step: start with one region this quarter, tracked against the $384K run-rate and the $20,573 trigger.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: approve at honest size (не reject, не «пять миллионов»).
2. ≥2 цифры, **якорь: 384,000-vs-4.86M ИЛИ v*=20,573**.
3. Названо, что наивный фрейм считает только сторону продажи (одна сторона цикла).

steps.answer_explain: **Answer: 5263200** — 12,000×255 + 12,000×0.72×255 = 3,060,000 + 2,203,200. Δ = **+$1,543,200 за 2 года** — отмена аккретивна. Направление CFO подтверждено полной моделью (величина иная: не «2.7M отдано», а 1.54M/2yr создано).

parts.prompt: Marketing pushes back: "The discounted volume gives us SCALE — 8,000 fewer customers spreads our fixed costs thinner." What do you check before accepting or dismissing this?
parts.model_answer: Whether total fixed cost actually changes with volume — if it's fixed, per-policy allocation is cosmetic and no cash is lost.
parts.validation: credit if distinguishes total fixed vs per-policy allocation OR asks whether fixed costs change with volume. On credit → reveal Exhibit 2. On fail → L1 ("Does the platform bill go up when a customer leaves?"), then reveal and proceed.
parts.fallback: on elicitation fail: show hint_L1, then reveal and proceed

options.text: Yes — fixed costs per policy rise
No — total fixed cost is unchanged; the per-policy rise is an allocation artifact, not a cash effect
Not enough information

parts.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 2. Scent проверен и отклонён С МЕХАНИЗМОМ. **Validation:** choice == 2.

steps.prompt: Robustness: at what number of full-price joiners would killing the discount be exactly value-neutral over 2 years? Nearest whole number.
steps.answer_explain:
**Answer: 8482**
- Same full model: per full-price customer 2-yr value = 255×(1+0.72) = 438.60 → j* = 3,720,000 / 438.60 = 8,481.5 → **8,482**. Marketing прогнозирует 12,000 — запас 41%. Решение robust. **[9b].**

steps.prompt: Your conclusion on killing the discount:

options.text: No — the scale loss reverses the saving
No — losing 8,000 customers outweighs the margin gain
Yes — the full cohort model agrees with the CFO's direction: +$1.54M over two years, robust down to 8,482 joiners, and the scale objection is an allocation artifact
Not enough information

steps.answer_explain: **Answer:** option 3. Оба соблазнительных «No» — ложные развороты, отклоняются с цифрами. **Validation:** choice == 3.

steps.prompt: In one line: why can you be confident this recommendation won't reverse under scrutiny?
steps.model_answer: The naive discount-line view and the full 2-year cohort model agree in direction (+2.7M claimed, +1.54M true), and both candidate reversals — the scale argument (fixed cost unchanged) and the volume loss (breakeven at 8,482 vs 12,000 expected) — fail when checked.
steps.validation: credit if (1) both views agree AND (2) ≥1 checked-and-cleared reversal mechanism named.

steps.prompt: "Record your recommendation to Quindale's CEO — 60–90 seconds."
steps.model_answer: Quindale should kill the new-customer discount — and I can confirm the CFO's instinct survives the full test, though the true prize is different from his number. The discount line says $2.7 million given away; the honest two-year cohort model says killing it creates $1,543,200 — because the discount buys bad business: those customers claim $640 against $585 and renew at 55 against 72 percent, so a full-price joiner is worth $438.60 over two years against far less for a discounted one. I checked both ways this could reverse. First, the scale argument: our $3 million of platform cost doesn't change with volume — the per-policy rise from $20 to $21.13 is an allocation artifact, not cash. Second, the volume risk: the decision stays positive as long as at least 8,482 of the 20,000 still join at full price, and marketing projects 12,000 — a 41% cushion. So: implement, and redirect part of the discount budget into retention of the full-price cohort. Risks: internally, marketing's 12,000 projection is the weak joint — validate with a two-region pilot before national rollout; externally, a competitor may weaponize our removed discount in acquisition ads, so brand should pre-position the value message. Next step: the two-region pilot next quarter with cohort-quality tracking.
steps.checklist:
Credit if ALL of:
1. Recommendation first: kill the discount / implement.
2. **Clean-якорь: подтверждение ОБОИХ путей** — наивное направление CFO И полная когортная модель сходятся (2.7M-vs-1,543,200 в любом виде).
3. ≥1 проверенный-и-отклонённый разворот с цифрой: scale/allocation ($20→$21.13, fixed unchanged) ИЛИ j*=8,482-vs-12,000.
4. ≥1 internal + ≥1 external риск; ≥1 time-bound next step.
Fail если кандидат «нашёл» разворот, которого нет, или ни одного проверенного механизма, или вывод в конце.
